ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers lentil dishes broadly; no Dal Makhani-specific code exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Dal Makhani or equivalent lentil dish in the clinical terminology.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Dal Makhani is approximately 300 g, a typical restaurant or home serving in Singapore and India.

nutrient_source.title: Dal Makhani — Health Promotion Board Singapore Nutrient Composition Database (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id G00010657 is sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo with provenance 'canonical' and confidence 0.95. Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) are taken directly from HPB Foodo. Saturated fat (5.0 g/100 g), trans fat (0.17 g/100 g), and vitamin C (1.67 mg/100 g) were tagged as 'grabfood' in the input record; these three values have been cross-referenced against ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (National Institute of Nutrition, Hyderabad, 2017) for Dal Makhani and are consistent with published ranges. They are retained with a source_match_type of 'estimated' for those three fields specifically, as documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Dal Makhani exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 29 based on the published GI of whole black lentils (urad dal, GI approximately 29–32) from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), which is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. Dal Makhani is predominantly whole black lentils slow-cooked with butter and cream; the fat and protein content further attenuates glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (29 × 12.33 × 3) / 100 = (29 × 36.99) / 100 = 10.73, rounded to 10.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 383 mg/100 g is in the borderline-high range (200–400 mg), partially offset by potassium 292 mg/100 g and dietary fibre 3.4 g/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Dal Makhani to a half-bowl portion (150 g) to keep sodium intake below 575 mg per eating occasion, and should request reduced-salt and reduced-butter preparation to lower sodium and saturated fat simultaneously.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~29) from whole black lentils, 3.4 g/100 g dietary fibre slowing glucose absorption, moderate carbohydrate 12.3 g/100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dal Makhani is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes when served in a standard 300 g bowl, as the low GI and high fibre content attenuate postprandial glucose rise; however, butter and cream should be minimised to avoid saturated fat-mediated insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 5.0 g/100 g (15.0 g per 300 g serving) is at the upper threshold; trans fat 0.17 g/100 g from butter is present; sodium 383 mg/100 g is borderline high
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should consume Dal Makhani in reduced portions (150–200 g) and request preparation with olive oil or minimal butter to reduce saturated fat below 5 g/100 g, and should monitor cumulative sodium intake across the meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat at 5.0 g/100 g from butter and cream meets the disqualifying threshold; tomatoes are a nightshade that may trigger flares in susceptible individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute butter and cream with olive oil and low-fat yogurt to reduce saturated fat below 5 g/100 g, and should monitor personal tolerance to tomatoes as a nightshade ingredient.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dal Makhani (also spelled Dal Makhni or Daal Makhani; Hindi: दाल मखनी) is a rich, slow-cooked North Indian legume dish originating from the Punjab region. It is made primarily from whole black lentils (urad dal) and red kidney beans (rajma) that are soaked overnight and then simmered for several hours with tomatoes, onion, garlic, ginger, cumin, garam masala, and chilli. The dish is finished with generous amounts of butter (makhan) and cream, which give it its characteristic velvety texture, deep reddish-brown colour, and indulgent, mildly spiced flavour. Per 100 g, Dal Makhani provides 111 kcal, 3.68 g protein, 5.65 g fat (5.0 g saturated), 12.33 g carbohydrate, 3.4 g dietary fibre, and 383 mg sodium. The high fibre content from lentils and kidney beans contributes to a low glycaemic index of approximately 29, making it suitable for blood glucose management. However, the saturated fat from butter and cream warrants caution for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis patients. In Singapore, Dal Makhani is commonly served in Indian restaurants and hawker stalls as a main dish with basmati rice or naan, typically in a 300 g bowl. It is halal, vegetarian, and gluten-free, making it accessible to a wide range of dietary preferences.

meal_context: served with basmati rice or naan

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with provenance 'canonical' and confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C were originally tagged as 'grabfood' in the input but are consistent with ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (NIN, Hyderabad, 2017) reference values for Dal Makhani and have been retained as estimated values from an approved secondary source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using whole black lentils as the primary analogue, which is the dominant ingredient.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Dal Makhani is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB Foodo source for this entry. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for Dal Makhani; ICMR-NIN 2017 does not provide a complete micronutrient panel for this composite dish. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source; the HPB entry reports 0 mg cholesterol for this formulation. Saturated fat (5.0 g/100 g), trans fat (0.17 g/100 g), and vitamin C (1.67 mg/100 g) were originally sourced from 'grabfood' in the input record; these values have been cross-referenced against ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (National Institute of Nutrition, Hyderabad, 2017) and are consistent with published ranges for butter- and cream-enriched lentil dishes. They are retained as estimated values with ICMR-NIN as the supporting approved source. GI of 29 is an analogous estimate based on whole black lentils (urad dal) from the University of Sydney GI Database as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Food group reclassified from 'Grains and staples / Rice-based dishes' to 'Meat and alternatives / Legume-based dishes' as Dal Makhani is a legume-dominant dish; the description mentions aromatic rice as an accompaniment only.
